title: Opening and the Blues at the Smithsonian
summary: David Cline opens the April 2013 Los Angeles session, and Caldwell recalls teaching at Howard, working with Bernice Johnson Reagon, and talking blues with Alan Lomax and Bill Barlow.

title: A Grandfather Who Was a Power Broker
summary: Caldwell traces his family of migrant farmers and his light-skinned grandfather, an uneducated man who bought property, knew the mayor, and worked as a segregated-theater projectionist.

title: Deming, a Border Crossroads
summary: He describes Deming, New Mexico as a crossroads of Pancho Villa, Geronimo, Cochise, and the Butterfield Overland Mail, a place of constant influx and Boy Scout stories.

title: The Colored Baseball Team
summary: His grandfather ran a barnstorming colored baseball team of Indian, Asian, and Black players across the Southwest, while his father, a train foreman, had reached Marquette.

title: Storytellers and the Boxcar
summary: Caldwell recalls storytellers who performed the Signifying Monkey and once killed a rattlesnake mid-tale, and his early life lived partly in a railroad boxcar.

title: Born in 1945, Around the Movies
summary: Born in 1945, Caldwell spent his early years with his grandfather and was always around the movie world as his grandfather projected drive-in films.

title: The Only Black Child in the White School
summary: By an accident of which side of the railroad track he lived on, Caldwell became the only Black child at the all-white Smith School, was pelted with rocks, then learned to fight and became a jock.

title: A Proud Family Stands Up
summary: With about 150 Black migrants in town, Caldwell's father taught his children pride and never to cow-tail to anyone over race, a lesson Caldwell passed on to his siblings.

title: Cowboys, Police, and a Town's Protection
topic: Standing Up in a Pioneer Town
summary: Caldwell recounts his brothers proving themselves to cowboys and his sister fighting a New Mexico cop, after which the hometown judge, knowing the respected family, let them off, an outcome he says would have meant prison or death elsewhere.

title: Photography, the Desert, and the Draft
summary: After six months at an Arizona art school, Caldwell was drafted, and on returning began photographing his family and the desert, seeing Black skin against the earth.

title: Vietnam Strips Away the Veil
summary: Vietnam pulled the veil from Caldwell's eyes and ended his religiousness when preachers sanctioned killing; he saw shared humanity in the Montagnards, read Malcolm X, and reflected on pride mislabeled as militancy.

title: Trapped in the Army, Refusing the Knife
summary: Hating to be trapped, Caldwell refused exploratory knee surgery, was made an ambulatory patient, and was shipped to Japan.

title: Learning Bureaucracy in Japan
summary: In Japan he sought out power, eating Thanksgiving with the American ambassador and Christmas with the base general, then used that schooling when shipped back to Vietnam during the Tet Offensive.

title: A Pacifist in the War Zone
summary: During Tet, Caldwell's unit pulled an old man in, refused an order to kill him, and walked nearly 150 villagers to safety; he resolved to become a pacifist in a war zone, killing only to survive.

title: Additive Paint and Phoenix College Football
summary: Out of the military, Caldwell added paint to his photographs of Black bodies in the desert, work that won him a full ride to UCLA, and recalls proving himself as a fullback at Phoenix College while refusing to be called boy.

title: Quitting Economics for Revolution
summary: He abandoned economics when it could not predict the depression it studied, turning to still photography and a revolutionary-philosophy class taught by a defected KGB agent.

title: The Artist Who Crosses Every Border
summary: Caldwell argues the artist survives every revolution because art, unlike politics, lets people communicate across borders, a lesson he carried teaching Nazis at CalArts and entering Cuba and Ghana as a spiritual person.

title: Art as a Political Act
summary: Pressed on whether art is still political, Caldwell explains he runs his space like television, renting to everyone, even anti-police events, refusing the police's demand for an understanding.

title: Founding the Community Video Workshop
summary: Returning to Los Angeles after Howard, Caldwell won a grant as one of the state's first video artists and ran workshops in Shirley Clarke's media-democracy spirit, putting cameras in children's hands.

title: The Fused Art World of 1970s Watts
summary: Caldwell describes the holistic Los Angeles art scene he found in 1971 around Watts and John Outterbridge: fused musicians, poets, and painters, African co-ops, drum classes, and the Crenshaw performers that drew him in.

title: Los Angeles and the History of Forgetting
summary: Using his ethnographic instinct, Caldwell uncovered Los Angeles as a place colonized by Black Hispanics, the Calle de los Negros, and what Norman Klein called the history of forgetting, which became his banner.

title: Electronic Cafe and Global Black History
summary: From the 1984 Electronic Cafe that linked communities through early internet, Caldwell pursued the confluence of cultures and a global Black history of Calafia, Black Moors, the Phoenicians, and Hannibal's Carthage.

part: Hip-Hop, Film, and the L.A. Rebellion
title: Project Blowed and Hip-Hop as Remix
summary: Caldwell founded KAOS Network and worked with young people, framing hip-hop as historical remix and homage, introducing kids to James Brown even as they scratched up his albums.

title: Filming the History of African People
summary: He describes his films Babylon Is Falling, Medea, and the chant-driven work compressing the history of African people into minutes, fusing animation, Amiri Baraka's poetry, and a heartbeat into visual ritual.

title: I and I, Capoeira, and the Nubian
summary: Caldwell explains his film I & I on the making of the other, his drive to infuse soul into film's rhythm, capoeira as a coded martial art, and his planned Nubian series tracing the unwritten western migration.

title: Against Blaxploitation
summary: Caldwell distinguishes true stories like Superfly, Shaft, and Sweet Sweetback from the imitations he calls blaxploitation, work that exploits conditions society itself created.

title: Music as Cosmology
summary: He argues that Black music is a hologram of African cosmology seeded by the Egyptians, and that the great migrations followed invasions by figures like Alexander and Caesar.

title: The UCLA Cohort and the L.A. Rebellion
summary: As the first film students of color in UCLA's regular program, treated as ethnographic others, Caldwell and about thirteen peers gathered around Haile Gerima, won and lost the grants, and produced some fifty-five films.

title: Naming the Rebellion, Embraced in Europe
summary: Caldwell reflects on the L.A. Rebellion name, the group's stand against blaxploitation, and how their work was celebrated in Europe while shown only in American museums and colleges.

title: Repurposing Payphones With Sankofa
summary: Caldwell describes his current Sankofa-principled work repurposing payphones into Wi-Fi totems and computers, developed with a diverse class at the School of Innovation to serve the poor and vulnerable.

title: Occupying the Virtual Space
summary: He frames the project as occupying virtual space with Black iconography to resist gentrification, hacking a privately owned payphone in front of his building and meeting LA Metro to help define what they dream toward.

title: Mapping the Neighborhood's Genius
summary: Caldwell shows the label and Phoenix logo, QR-coded payphone ideas, and a map of the cultural density of a five-mile radius where nearly every famous Black figure once lived or worked, an economic engine he wants recognized.

title: Phantom Galleries and Empty Buildings
summary: He showcases cutting-edge young musicians and religious hip-hop at his space and the phantom-galleries effort that filled nine empty buildings with art and helped upgrade a derelict neighborhood theater.

title: Cuba, the Babalawos, and an Army of Healers
summary: In 1999 Caldwell took Black yoga teachers to Cuba to tap cultural memory through the Babalawos, learning of immunization-based body markings studied by physicists and a society that sent healers and 300,000 soldiers against apartheid.

title: Cuban Music and the Limits of Socialism
summary: Caldwell recalls Los Munequitos de Matanzas, watching hip-hop age across eighteen years, and the heavy bureaucracy of Cuban socialism that nonetheless guaranteed school, healthcare, and an enriched artistic life.

title: The Rodney King Verdict and the Uprising
summary: Caldwell describes the shock of the Rodney King acquittal and the multicultural rage that followed, the anarchy of broad-daylight looting, and his suspicion at how thoroughly the buildings burned.

title: Saving the Gallery, the Korean Community
summary: He recounts neighbors saving a Black gallery of African sculpture from fire and the complicated standing of Korean families, whom he saw as the Black people of Asia, tied to Black soldiers from Vietnam.

title: The Emmett Till Parallel and the Gang Myth
summary: Drawing on a study, Caldwell compares the uprising's spark to the whisper that killed Emmett Till and argues the gang and crack epidemics were too organized and well armed to be homegrown, pointing to outside agents.

title: Refusing to Film Degradation
topic: Art Over the Documentation of Pain
summary: Caldwell explains he cannot film people in states of degradation, choosing instead works like Word Sound and Power that make flowers from compost, and his lifelong distance from a communism he found bureaucratic and racist, before the closing credit.
